document_type.name: Generic Document
document_type.description: Any PDF document. Extract all visible sections, subsections, tables, lists, and content into structured JSON.

prompt: Extract document header/metadata information from this first page.
Look for ANY metadata typically found in document headers, title pages, or footers:
- Title or heading of the document
- Organisation, company, department, or agency name
1. Extract the EXACT text as it appears - do not paraphrase.
2. If a field is not found, use an empty string: ""
3. Look in: page headers, footers, title blocks, cover pages, front matter.
Return ONLY the JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):

definitions.section: A major section of the document with a distinct heading. Contains an ordered sequence of content blocks (paragraphs, tables, subsections) preserving original document order.
definitions.unhandled_content: Content that does not clearly belong to any other section type, or boilerplate such as disclaimers, signatures, blank pages.

terms: Rate escalation OR Price adjustment / amendment OR Fee escalation / Fee revision OR Indexation OR Rise / Fall OR Markups OR Revised rates / Pricing OR Increase / Escalation of charges OR Price escalation OR Indexed price adjustment OR Cost escalation adjustment OR Annual uplift OR CPI adjustment OR Cost index adjustment OR Escalation factor

Agreed pricing OR Rate Card / Service fee / Contract price OR Negotiated rate OR Lump sum OR Fixed price OR Basis of charge OR Contracted prices OR Accepted rates OR Confirmed pricing OR Agreed rates OR Confirmed pricing OR Agreed rates OR Base pricing
Preamble OR Preliminary / Preparatory statement OR introduction OR Introductory provisions OR Purpose OR Recitals OR Background OR Recitals OR Context
Permutation of cost OR Cost variations / Adjustment OR Price variation
Price mechanism OR Pricing scheme / schedule OR Fee schedule / Schedule of rates OR Pricing mechanism OR Basis of pricing OR Commercial arrangements Or Pricing methodology
Price on ad/hoc basis OR Ad-Hoc pricing OR As-needed / on-request pricing OR Case-by-case pricing OR Time and materials pricing OR Variable pricing OR On demand pricing
Currency OR AUD OR USD OR Dollar amount OR Payment denomination OR Base / specified currency OR Monetary unit OR Contract currency OR Payment currency OR Exchange currency

Mobilise and demobilise OR Commence work OR Deploy OR begin delivery OR Initiate activities OR Site works OR Start up activities OR Ramp-up OR Cease work / Withdraw / Complete / Close out / Wrap up / Removal / Termination OR Commencement OR Close out OR EStablishment OR Deployment OR Transition
Price components OR Cost components OR Price / Cost elements OR Cost breakdown OR Fee components OR Refer to agreed pricing above
The basis on which the charges should be applied OR charging methodology OR Fee calculation / Model / Mechanism OR Application of charges
Any clauses that amend the agreed rates OR Rate variation / amendment / adjustment clauses OR Conditional OR Rate adjustment OR Pricing adjustment OR Fee amendment OR Pricing modification

template: You are a document verification expert.
I am showing you {n_images} page image(s) from a document (page {page_numbers}).
Carefully examine the page image(s) and determine whether the information
above is CORRECT, INCORRECT, MISSING, or UNVERIFIABLE.
I am sending you {n_images} consecutive document page images.
They are numbered Image 1 through Image {n_images}.
Before classifying anything, scan ALL images and identify the
visual hierarchy of headings by examining these cues:
Font weight           | Bold / ALL-CAPS              | Regular or lighter bold
Numbering depth       | 1, 2, 3 or I, II, III       | 1.1, 1.2, 2.1 or a, b, c
Page position         | Often starts a new page      | Continues on same page
Spacing               | Large space above/below      | Less space above/below
Identify the TOP-LEVEL heading style first. Only headings matching that
top-level style are SECTIONS. Everything nested below them is a subsection.
(a) Does it use the SAME visual style as the top-level headings?
(b) Does it appear BETWEEN two top-level headings (i.e., nested)?
(c) Is its numbering a SUB-number of a top-level heading?
If (a)=YES and (b)=NO  -> it IS a section  -> include it.
If (b)=YES or (c)=YES  -> it is a subsection -> do NOT include it.
If ambiguous            -> do NOT include it.
- Extract the EXACT heading text from the document.
- Use IMAGE NUMBERS (1 to {n_images}), NOT printed page numbers.
YOUR TASK: Identify ONLY top-level SECTIONS in the {n_images} images.
Return ONLY a JSON array (no markdown fences, no commentary):
- All images must be covered -- no gaps, no overlaps.
- Err on the side of fewer, larger sections.

system_preamble: You are an expert data extraction agent. Your ONLY job is to copy text EXACTLY as it appears in the document images.
=== ABSOLUTE RULES (never violate) ===
1. VERBATIM COPY: Every word, number, and punctuation mark must match the source exactly.
- Do NOT fix spelling, grammar, or capitalisation.
- Do NOT rephrase, paraphrase, summarise, or reword.
- Do NOT add or drop words.
2. CHARACTER ENCODING: Output ONLY ASCII characters (codes 0-127).
- Replace unicode dashes with ' - ', bullets with '- ', smart quotes with straight quotes.
3. COMPLETENESS: Extract ALL visible text from EVERY page shown.
4. JSON OUTPUT:
- Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown fences, no commentary.
- Start your response with {{ or [.
- Empty fields: '' for strings, [] for arrays.
=== OUTPUT SCHEMA ===
You MUST return a JSON object with this structure:
"heading": "<exact section heading>",
"heading_level": "<1 for top-level, 2 for sub, etc>",
"content": [
// An ORDERED array of content blocks, in DOCUMENT READING ORDER.
// Each block is one of three types:
// TYPE 1: paragraph
{{ "type": "paragraph", "text": "<exact paragraph text>" }},
// TYPE 2: table
{{ "type": "table", "caption": "<if any>", "headers": ["col1", "col2"], "rows": [["cell", "cell"]] }},
// TYPE 3: subsection (can nest further content blocks)
{{ "type": "subsection", "heading": "<sub-heading text>", "heading_level": "<level>", "content": [
// ... same block types recursively ...
CRITICAL ORDERING RULE:
The 'content' array MUST preserve the exact reading order from the document.
If a paragraph appears before a table on the page, the paragraph block MUST
come before the table block in the array. If a subsection appears between
two paragraphs, it MUST appear between them in the array.
DO NOT group all paragraphs together and all tables together.
DO NOT separate body text from subsections.
Preserve the EXACT document order in the content array.
default_template: Extract ALL text from the document pages shown into the JSON schema above.
Section: {section_name} ({section_type})
Pages: {start_page} to {end_page}
Next section after this one: '{next_section_name}'
{image_info}
{doc_type_guidance}
=== WHAT TO EXTRACT ===
Extract ALL visible text from page {start_page} through page {end_page}.
STOP extracting when you reach content that belongs to '{next_section_name}'.
=== HEADING ===
- 'heading': The EXACT section heading text, copied verbatim.
- 'heading_level': '1' for top-level, '2' for sub-section, etc.
=== CONTENT ARRAY (critical) ===
The 'content' array is an ORDERED list of content blocks in reading order:
PARAGRAPH blocks (type: 'paragraph'):
- One block per logical paragraph, numbered clause, or bullet point.
- Include the number/letter prefix as part of the text.
- If a sentence wraps across lines, join into one text string.
- Standalone lines (notes, labels, dates) are separate paragraph blocks.
TABLE blocks (type: 'table'):
- caption (if any), headers (array of column names), rows (array of arrays).
- Copy cell text exactly. Merged cells: text in first position, '' for spanned.
SUBSECTION blocks (type: 'subsection'):
- When you see a sub-heading, create a subsection block with its own content array.
- Apply the same rules recursively inside the subsection's content.
=== ORDERING ===
The content array MUST match the document's reading order exactly.
A paragraph that appears between two tables on the page must appear
between those table blocks in the array.
=== MULTI-PAGE SECTIONS ===
- Process ALL pages from {start_page} to {end_page} sequentially.
- Content flowing across pages is part of the same block.
- Exclude page headers and footers.
=== SELF-CHECK ===
Before returning, verify:
1. Every page ({start_page} through {end_page}) has been extracted.
2. Content blocks are in exact reading order.
3. Every string is verbatim (no rewording).
4. Stopped before '{next_section_name}'.
Return the complete JSON now (start with {{, no markdown):
general_rules: - Every word must be copied verbatim from the document.
- Numbered items like (a), (b), (c) are separate paragraph blocks.
- The content array must be in exact document reading order.
- Check every page: did you extract all content from pages {start_page} through {end_page}?

review_template: You are a document quality reviewer. Compare the extracted text against the page images.
Return ONLY valid JSON with WORD_ACCURACY, MISSING_INFORMATION_ACCURACY, DUPLICATION_ACCURACY.

template: You are a document analysis expert.
For each group of terms, extract the relevant sections along with the rationale. The extracted sections must include details on how the pricing tables should be updated or amended.
Return ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):
You are a contract analysis expert.
I will give you the extracted sections of a document.
Your task is to identify the EFFECTIVE DATE -- the date from
which the contract or variation should apply.
WHAT TO LOOK FOR (in priority order):
or 'Start Date' defined in the contract terms.
2. The execution or signing date of the contract or variation.
3. A date derived from context -- e.g. 'this Deed takes effect
from the Commencement Date defined in clause X', or a date
that can be read from a variation's terms.
4. Any date that indicates when the agreement's terms begin
- execution_date: The date the document was signed/executed
- variation_date: The date a variation takes effect
- other: Any other date that serves as the effective date
Return ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):
"reason": "<why this could be the effective date>"
- primary_effective_date = the MOST LIKELY effective date.
- all_dates_found = ALL candidate dates, even low confidence.
- If no date can be identified, set no_date_found to true.
- Use the EXACT date text as it appears in the document.
You are a contract pricing analysis expert.
Identify every UNIT OF MEASURE used for pricing.
Return ONLY a JSON object with units_of_measure, distinct_units, no_uom_found.

image_description_prompt: Analyze this image from the '{section_name}' section.

image_context_hints._default: Describe this image briefly, noting any text, labels, or key visual elements.
